===== dot_ssh/create_private_config =====
# ~/.ssh/config
#
# Managed by chezmoi as create-only:
# - Created only when this file does not exist.
# - Existing ~/.ssh/config will NOT be overwritten.
#
# Put host-specific entries in ~/.ssh/config.d/*. Files in that directory are
# loaded in lexical order.
Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*

Host *
    # Conservative cross-platform keepalive defaults.
    ServerAliveInterval 60
    ServerAliveCountMax 3

    # SSH agent selection is handled automatically at the shell level by
    # ~/.config/zsh/tools/94_ssh_agent.zsh (Bitwarden → existing → ssh-agent).
    # Only uncomment an IdentityAgent below if you need config-level pinning
    # (e.g. for cron jobs or non-interactive contexts without shell init).
    #
    # macOS (.dmg):
    # IdentityAgent ~/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock
    # macOS (App Store):
    # IdentityAgent ~/Library/Containers/com.bitwarden.desktop/Data/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock
    # Linux native/.deb:
    # IdentityAgent ~/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock
    # Linux Snap:
    # IdentityAgent ~/snap/bitwarden/current/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock
    # Linux Flatpak:
    # IdentityAgent ~/.var/app/com.bitwarden.desktop/data/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock

===== dot_ssh/private_config.d/create_private_00-defaults =====
# ~/.ssh/config.d/00-defaults
#
# Stub file for optional global defaults.
# Intentionally all-commented to avoid duplicating Host * defaults from
# ~/.ssh/config.

# Host *
#     AddKeysToAgent yes
#     IdentitiesOnly yes
#     PreferredAuthentications publickey

===== dot_ssh/private_config.d/create_private_01_git =====
# ~/.ssh/config.d/git
#
# Public host defaults for Git providers.
#
# GitHub uses ssh.github.com:443 as an alternative SSH endpoint.
# This is more reliable in corporate/restricted networks where port 22
# may be blocked or filtered. See: https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/
# troubleshooting-ssh/using-ssh-over-the-https-port

Host github.com
    HostName ssh.github.com
    User git
    Port 443

Host gitlab.com
    HostName gitlab.com
    User git
    # Uncomment for SOCKS5 proxy (e.g. behind GFW):
    # ProxyCommand nc -X 5 -x 127.0.0.1:7890 %h %p

# Example: multiple account alias (disabled by default)
# Host github-work
#     HostName github.com
#     User git
#     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/github_work.pub
#     IdentitiesOnly yes

# Example: Bitwarden agent pinning (disabled by default)
# Host gitlab-work
#     HostName gitlab.com
#     User git
#     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/gitlab_work.pub
#     IdentitiesOnly yes

```text
===== 94_ssh_agent.zsh =====
# Bitwarden SSH Agent

Use the Bitwarden desktop app as your SSH agent so private keys never leave the vault.

- **Official docs**: <https://bitwarden.com/help/ssh-agent/>
- **Requires**: Bitwarden desktop >= 2025.1.2, SSH agent enabled in **Settings > Enable SSH agent**

## 0. Install Bitwarden Desktop

### Automated (via this repo)

When `installBitwarden` is enabled during `chezmoi init`, the ansible bitwarden role automatically installs:
- **CLI** (`bw`): via npm on all profiles
- **Desktop app**: on desktop profiles only (`ubuntu_desktop`, `macos`)
  - **macOS**: via Homebrew Cask (`brew install --cask bitwarden`)
  - **Ubuntu**: via Snap (primary) or `.deb` download (fallback)

```bash
# Enable during chezmoi init (if not already)
chezmoi init --force  # Answer "y" to "Install Bitwarden CLI" prompt

# Or run the bitwarden role manually with desktop install
cd ~/.ansible && ansible-playbook playbooks/linux.yml --tags bitwarden --extra-vars "bitwarden_install_desktop=true"
```

### Manual

If you prefer to install manually or are on `ubuntu_server`:
- **macOS**: `brew install --cask bitwarden`
- **Ubuntu (snap)**: `sudo snap install bitwarden`
- **Ubuntu (.deb)**: Download from [vault.bitwarden.com](https://vault.bitwarden.com/download/?app=desktop&platform=linux&variant=deb)
- **Flatpak**: `flatpak install flathub com.bitwarden.desktop`

## How It Works

```
┌──────────────┐     SSH_AUTH_SOCK      ┌───────────────────┐
│  ssh / git   │ ───────────────────▶   │  Bitwarden Agent  │
│  (any CLI)   │                        │  (desktop app)    │
└──────────────┘                        └───────────────────┘
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                        Vault (encrypted)
                                        ├─ SSH: ssh_key_1
                                        ├─ SSH: ssh_key_2
                                        └─ SSH: ssh_key_3
```

When an SSH client needs a key, it talks to whichever agent `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` points to.
Bitwarden creates a Unix socket file; pointing `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` there makes Bitwarden the agent.
The desktop app prompts you to authorize each key usage (configurable under Settings).

## 1. Import Keys Into Bitwarden

### Via the desktop app

1. Open Bitwarden desktop, click **New > SSH key**.
2. Paste your private key (OpenSSH or PKCS#8 format) using **Import key from clipboard**.
3. The public key and fingerprint are derived automatically.

### Via the CLI script

This repo includes an import script (see [import_ssh_to_bw.sh docs](../this_repo/scripts/import_ssh_to_bw.sh.md)):

```bash
# Interactive -- pick which keys to import
ssh-to-bitwarden

# Import all keys from ~/.ssh
ssh-to-bitwarden --all

# Preview only
ssh-to-bitwarden --dry-run
```

## 2. Configure SSH_AUTH_SOCK

The socket path depends on how Bitwarden was installed:

| Installation   | macOS socket path                                                                         | Linux socket path                                                       |
|----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `.dmg`         | `~/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock`                                                             | --                                                                      |
| Mac App Store  | `~/Library/Containers/com.bitwarden.desktop/Data/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock`               | --                                                                      |
| Native / .deb  | --                                                                                        | `~/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock`                                           |
| Snap           | --                                                                                        | `~/snap/bitwarden/current/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock`                    |
| Flatpak        | --                                                                                        | `~/.var/app/com.bitwarden.desktop/data/.bitwarden-ssh-agent.sock`       |

This repo auto-detects the socket in `~/.config/zsh/tools/94_ssh_agent.zsh`.
It tries each candidate path in order and exports `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` for the first socket found **and responding**.
No manual shell configuration is needed if you use the managed zsh config.

### Automatic Fallback

If Bitwarden desktop is not running (or its socket is stale), the agent script
automatically falls back to a persistent `ssh-agent` and loads keys from `~/.ssh/`.
See [SSH Agent Fallback](../tools/ssh-agent.md) for full details on the fallback chain.

## 3. Verify

```bash
# List keys the agent knows about
ssh-add -l

# Expected output (example):
# 3072 SHA256:S93TIv0W2E1B...  SSH: ssh_key_1 (RSA)
# 3072 SHA256:B7ZcVqELH0RQ...  SSH: ssh_key_2 (RSA)
# 3072 SHA256:i+mZs1/NiKo+...  SSH: ssh_key_3 (RSA)

# Test GitHub authentication
ssh -T git@github.com
# Hi daviddwlee84! You've successfully authenticated, ...
```

If `ssh-add -l` returns "The agent has no identities", check:

1. Bitwarden desktop is running and unlocked.
2. SSH agent is enabled in Bitwarden **Settings**.
3. `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` points to the correct socket (`echo $SSH_AUTH_SOCK`).

## 4. Interaction With ~/.ssh/config
Shell cwd was reset to /Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm working through the Windows-specific SSH configuration details. On Windows, OpenSSH maps the SSH config file to `%USERPROFILE%\.ssh\config`, and importantly, it does support the `Include` directive with glob patterns—forward slashes work fine in paths, and tilde expansion is handled properly even on Windows.

For file permissions, the `private_` attribute in chezmoi is mostly cosmetic on Windows since ACL handling is inconsistent, though SSH itself is strict about config file ownership and permissions. The key difference for Windows is the SSH agent setup: instead of Unix sockets, Windows uses named pipes like `\\.\pipe\openssh-ssh-agent` for both the built-in OpenSSH Authentication Agent service and Bitwarden's SSH agent implementation, so the `IdentityAgent` configuration needs to point to that named pipe path.

For proxy commands on Windows, `nc` isn't typically available, so I'd need to use alternatives like `connect` from nmap or `ncat` with SOCKS5 proxy flags, or just leave that example commented out. Windows OpenSSH also supports `~` and forward slashes in Include paths, though older versions had issues with glob patterns that are mostly resolved in modern builds.

Now I'm thinking through the chezmoi naming convention for this Windows setup — mapping `create_private_config` under `dot_ssh/` to `~/.ssh/config`, and the config.d subdirectory files similarly with the `private_` prefix. I need to confirm that chezmoi's `private_` attribute actually works on Windows systems.

On Windows, chezmoi does apply ACLs for `private_` files (setting them to owner-only), which is beneficial since SSH can be picky about config file permissions and will reject overly-permissive settings. So keeping the `private_` prefix makes sense here. The `create_` template also works fine, and I'm now deciding whether these should be `.tmpl` files or stay as plain files like the parent repo uses.

Since this is Windows, the commented examples in the SSH config need to reflect Windows-specific socket paths (named pipes, Windows OpenSSH agent service, Bitwarden Windows pipe). The user's request is to adopt the SSH configuration approach from the parent repo, which is primarily about the `~/.ssh/config` structure and `config.d/` skeleton. I'm checking whether the Windows repo already has an ssh-agent profile fragment and whether Bitwarden is set up there. I need to verify the documentation structure and cross-file requirements — checking whether SSH config needs a docs mirror, reviewing the profile.d fragments and package list, examining the README's SSH section, and confirming that the .chezmoiignore doesn't exclude the SSH directory.
</details></think>

```text
# dotfiles-windows

Native **Windows + PowerShell 7** dotfiles, managed by [chezmoi](https://chezmoi.io).
A self-contained, Windows-only companion to the cross-platform (macOS/Linux)
dotfiles at **[daviddwlee84/dotfiles](https://github.com/daviddwlee84/dotfiles)** —
the PowerShell layer is written natively rather than ported from the POSIX shell config.

> **Which repo do I install from?**
>
> | Your machine | Use |
> |---|---|
> | **Native Windows** (PowerShell) | **this repo** (`dotfiles-windows`) |
> | macOS / Linux / WSL | [daviddwlee84/dotfiles](https://github.com/daviddwlee84/dotfiles) |

> Status: work in progress. See [`docs/`](docs/) for the full handbook (bilingual
> EN / 繁體中文) once built.

## Quick start

From a fresh Windows PowerShell (or pwsh) session:

```powershell
irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daviddwlee84/dotfiles-windows/main/bootstrap.ps1 | iex
```

This installs [scoop](https://scoop.sh) (CLI tools) + [winget](https://learn.microsoft.com/windows/package-manager/)
(GUI apps), PowerShell 7, [chezmoi](https://chezmoi.io) and [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/),
then applies the dotfiles.

## What you get

- **Shell**: PowerShell 7 with a modular `$PROFILE` (`~/.config/powershell/profile.d/*.ps1`).
- **Prompt**: [starship](https://starship.rs) (shared config with the macOS/Linux dotfiles).
- **CLI tools** (scoop): git, neovim, lazygit, zoxide, fzf, bat, eza, ripgrep, fd, gh, delta, jq, yazi, btop, mise, uv, node, bun.
- **AI agents**: Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex, GitHub Copilot CLI, SpecStory, Antigravity.
- **Editors**: VSCode, Cursor, Notepad++ (shared settings/keybindings).
- **Apps** (winget): Windows Terminal, Alacritty, Raycast, PowerToys, Steam.
- **`copilot-proxy`** tool series, rewritten as a native PowerShell module.

## Package manager
=== README section headers ===
1:# dotfiles-windows
18:## Quick start
30:## What you get
40:## Package manager
45:## Layout
58:## Manual dotfiles ops
71:## Roadmap & lessons learned
```
</details></tool-use>

## Layout

| Path | What |
|---|---|
| `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` | Init prompts (role + feature toggles). |
| `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` | `$PROFILE` loader. |
| `dot_config/powershell/profile.d/` | Modular shell fragments. |
| `dot_config/powershell/modules/Copilot/` | `copilot-proxy` PowerShell module. |
| `dot_config/starship.toml` | Prompt config. |
| `.chezmoiscripts/` | Package install + editor-overlay scripts. |
| `bootstrap.ps1` | One-line installer. |
| `docs/`, `mkdocs.yml` | Bilingual documentation site. |

## Manual dotfiles ops

```powershell
chezmoi diff          # preview changes
chezmoi apply         # apply
just upgrade-scoop     # upgrade CLI tools
just upgrade-winget    # upgrade GUI apps
```

Links: [{"title":"SSH Agent | Bitwarden Contributing Documentation","url":"https://contributing.bitwarden.com/architecture/deep-dives/ssh/agent/"},{"title":"Change in handling of IdentityAgent configuration between 8.6 and 8.9 ...","url":"https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1949"},{"title":"Bitwarden SSH agent in WSL2 via a systemd user service (socat ...","url":"https://gist.github.com/eliottness/f144341ee3952493468dcab94da66313"},{"title":"Windows SSH Agent hardcoded to named pipe #16123 - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/bitwarden/clients/issues/16123"}]

I ran searches for that query. Top findings and useful links (short summary + citations):

- Bitwarden’s SSH agent on Windows exposes a global Windows named pipe at \\.\pipe\openssh-ssh-agent (the desktop app can act as the agent). The Bitwarden docs describe this and note only one application can act as the pipe server, so the built‑in OpenSSH agent service must be disabled if it’s already using the pipe. ([contributing.bitwarden.com](https://contributing.bitwarden.com/architecture/deep-dives/ssh/agent/))

- The Windows OpenSSH client expects a named-pipe-style endpoint. Historically people put an IdentityAgent line in ~/.ssh/config like:
  IdentityAgent \\.\pipe\openssh-ssh-agent
  However OpenSSH on Windows changed behavior around 8.9: backslashes in config can be interpreted badly; using forward‑slash form (//./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent) or ensuring correct escaping is recommended. Also if IdentityAgent appears in config it can prevent the environment variable from being used. See the Win32-OpenSSH / PowerShell GitHub discussion. ([github.com](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1949))

- If you need Bitwarden’s agent available inside WSL2 or other Unix-like environments, common workarounds bridge the Windows named pipe to a Unix socket (examples use npiperelay + socat or similar). Several guides and gists show scripts and systemd service examples to create a $SSH_AUTH_SOCK that forwards to \\.\pipe\openssh-ssh-agent. ([gist.github.com](https://gist.github.com/eliottness/f144341ee3952493468dcab94da66313))

- Community issues and posts note interoperability quirks: Bitwarden currently uses the hardcoded pipe name \\.\pipe\openssh-ssh-agent (which can cause multi-user / multi-agent conflicts) and some clients (PuTTY, older tools) may need special config or adapters. ([github.com](https://github.com/bitwarden/clients/issues/16123))

- Provide step‑by‑step instructions to configure OpenSSH (ssh_config) on Windows, disable the Windows ssh-agent service, and enable/use Bitwarden’s agent (including a WSL bridging script). Which would you prefer?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-10 06:30:17Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```
# ~/.ssh/config
#
# Managed by chezmoi as create-only:
# - Created only when this file does not exist.
# - Existing ~/.ssh/config will NOT be overwritten. If you already have one, add
#   `Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*` to it by hand to pick up the managed snippets.
#
# On Windows, `~/.ssh/config` == `%USERPROFILE%\.ssh\config` and is read by both
# the built-in Windows OpenSSH client and the copy bundled with scoop's git.
# chezmoi writes this file owner-only (the `private_` attribute maps to a
# restrictive ACL) so OpenSSH does not reject it with "Bad owner or permissions".
#
# Put host-specific entries in ~/.ssh/config.d/*. Files there load in lexical
# order. `Include` with a glob needs a recent Windows OpenSSH (ships since
# Win10 1809 / OpenSSH 8.x); scoop's git bundles its own recent build.
Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*

Host *
    # Conservative cross-platform keepalive defaults.
    ServerAliveInterval 60
    ServerAliveCountMax 3

    # Agent selection on Windows is the OpenSSH Authentication Agent service, not
    # a SSH_AUTH_SOCK dance: set it to start automatically and load your keys --
    #   Set-Service ssh-agent -StartupType Automatic
    #   Start-Service ssh-agent
    #   ssh-add $HOME\.ssh\id_ed25519
    # The client then talks to the named pipe //./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent by
    # default, so no IdentityAgent line is needed.
    #
    # Only uncomment IdentityAgent below for config-level pinning (e.g. a
    # non-interactive context, or to point at Bitwarden desktop's agent -- it
    # serves the SAME pipe, so stop the Windows ssh-agent service first to avoid
    # a collision). Use forward slashes: OpenSSH >= 8.9 mishandles the backslash
    # form, and setting IdentityAgent disables the env-var fallback.
    #
    # IdentityAgent //./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent

```

```text

```

# ~/.ssh/config.d/01_git
#
# Public host defaults for Git providers.
#
# GitHub uses ssh.github.com:443 as an alternative SSH endpoint. This is more
# reliable in corporate/restricted networks where port 22 may be blocked or
# filtered. See: https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/troubleshooting-ssh/
# using-ssh-over-the-https-port

Host github.com
    HostName ssh.github.com
    User git
    Port 443

Host gitlab.com
    HostName gitlab.com
    User git
    # Uncomment for a SOCKS5 proxy (e.g. behind the GFW). Windows has no `nc`;
    # use ncat (`scoop install nmap`) or `connect`:
    # ProxyCommand ncat --proxy 127.0.0.1:7890 --proxy-type socks5 %h %p

# Example: multiple account alias (disabled by default)
# Host github-work
#     HostName github.com
#     User git
#     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/github_work.pub
#     IdentitiesOnly yes

# Example: Bitwarden agent pinning (disabled by default; see ~/.ssh/config)
# Host gitlab-work
#     HostName gitlab.com
#     User git
#     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/gitlab_work.pub
#     IdentitiesOnly yes

```

```text
===== TARGET TREE (.ssh) =====
~/.ssh
~/.ssh/config
~/.ssh/config.d
~/.ssh/config.d/01_git
~/.ssh/config.d/00-defaults
===== ~/.ssh/config =====
# ~/.ssh/config
#
# Managed by chezmoi as create-only:
# - Created only when this file does not exist.
# - Existing ~/.ssh/config will NOT be overwritten. If you already have one, add
#   `Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*` to it by hand to pick up the managed snippets.
#
# On Windows, `~/.ssh/config` == `%USERPROFILE%\.ssh\config` and is read by both
# the built-in Windows OpenSSH client and the copy bundled with scoop's git.
# chezmoi writes this file owner-only (the `private_` attribute maps to a
# restrictive ACL) so OpenSSH does not reject it with "Bad owner or permissions".
#
# Put host-specific entries in ~/.ssh/config.d/*. Files there load in lexical
# order. `Include` with a glob needs a recent Windows OpenSSH (ships since
# Win10 1809 / OpenSSH 8.x); scoop's git bundles its own recent build.
Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*

Host *
    # Conservative cross-platform keepalive defaults.
    ServerAliveInterval 60
    ServerAliveCountMax 3

    # Agent selection on Windows is the OpenSSH Authentication Agent service, not
    # a SSH_AUTH_SOCK dance: set it to start automatically and load your keys --
    #   Set-Service ssh-agent -StartupType Automatic
    #   Start-Service ssh-agent
    #   ssh-add $HOME\.ssh\id_ed25519
    # The client then talks to the named pipe //./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent by
    # default, so no IdentityAgent line is needed.
    #
    # Only uncomment IdentityAgent below for config-level pinning (e.g. a
    # non-interactive context, or to point at Bitwarden desktop's agent -- it
    # serves the SAME pipe, so stop the Windows ssh-agent service first to avoid
    # a collision). Use forward slashes: OpenSSH >= 8.9 mishandles the backslash
    # form, and setting IdentityAgent disables the env-var fallback.
    #
    # IdentityAgent //./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent
===== ~/.ssh/config.d/ listing =====
.rw-------@  264 david 10 Jul 14:30 00-defaults
.rw-------@ 1.1k david 10 Jul 14:30 01_git
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-10 06:32:43Z)**_

Done. I ported the parent repo's SSH-config approach into this Windows repo, adapted for Windows OpenSSH.

## What I added (`dot_ssh/`, all create-only)

| Source | Target | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `dot_ssh/create_private_config` | `~/.ssh/config` | `Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*` + `Host *` keepalives |
| `dot_ssh/private_config.d/create_private_00-defaults` | `~/.ssh/config.d/00-defaults` | commented global-defaults stub |
| `dot_ssh/private_config.d/create_private_01_git` | `~/.ssh/config.d/01_git` | GitHub via `ssh.github.com:443`, GitLab, commented multi-account/proxy examples |

Same design as the cross-platform repo — a **create-only** skeleton (won't clobber an existing `~/.ssh/config`) that `Include`s drop-in snippets from `config.d/`, with `private_` giving owner-only perms so Windows OpenSSH doesn't reject the config.

## Windows-specific adaptations (not straight copies)
- **Agent**: replaced the macOS/Linux `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` + Bitwarden-socket guidance with the Windows **OpenSSH Authentication Agent service** and the `//./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent` named pipe. Noted that Bitwarden serves the *same* pipe (stop the service first to avoid collision) and to use forward-slashes because OpenSSH ≥8.9 mishandles the backslash form — [confirmed via Bitwarden's docs / Win32-OpenSSH #1949](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1949).
- **Proxy**: swapped the GFW `nc -X 5` example for `ncat --proxy … --proxy-type socks5` (Windows has no `nc`).
- Fixed a stale header the parent carried: its `01_git` file was labelled `config.d/git`; mine matches the real chezmoi target.

## Validation & bookkeeping
- Ran the repo's **isolated-apply idiom** (chezmoi has no Windows box here) — produced exactly `~/.ssh/{config, config.d/00-defaults, config.d/01_git}` with `.rw-------` perms. Cleaned up the temp dir.
- **README**: added a `dot_ssh/` Layout row + a short "SSH" section.
- **TODO.md**: recorded the one thing I deliberately *didn't* port — a Windows ssh-agent profile fragment (the parallel to the parent's `94_ssh_agent.zsh`) — as a P3 `[S]` item; validator passes.

The files are `create_`-only plain text (no `.tmpl`), so there are no init-prompt/CI-flag or template-render implications. Nothing is committed — staged changes are `dot_ssh/`, `README.md`, `TODO.md`.

Sources: [Bitwarden SSH agent architecture](https://contributing.bitwarden.com/architecture/deep-dives/ssh/agent/), [Win32-OpenSSH IdentityAgent handling](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1949)

# ===========================================================================
Ensure-Scoop

# Core CLI toolset — the shell baseline, always installed.
Scoop-Install @(
    'git', 'gh', '7zip', 'make', 'just',
    'starship', 'zoxide', 'fzf', 'fd', 'ripgrep', 'bat', 'eza', 'jq',
    'delta', 'lazygit', 'neovim', 'yazi', 'btop', 'television',
    'uv', 'gnupg',
    # Runtimes are NATIVE via scoop, not mise: mise's activate/shims are
    # unreliable on Windows and it has no windows-arm64 bun build. node + bun
    # here; Python via uv (below); go/rust/ruby under Extra runtimes.
    # copilot-proxy needs bun/bunx.
    'nodejs-lts', 'bun',
    # LazyVim needs a C compiler for tree-sitter parsers (zig), the tree-sitter
    # CLI the nvim-treesitter `main` branch requires, and a clipboard provider so
    # `unnamedplus` yanks reach the Windows clipboard (win32yank).
    'zig', 'tree-sitter', 'win32yank'
)

# On Windows-on-ARM, scoop's neovim is amd64 (runs under x64 emulation) while
# zig installs arm64-native, so zig-built tree-sitter parsers are the wrong
=== az gate context (265-282) ===

{{ if .installLlmTools -}}
# Local LLM tools: Ollama (winget) + LiteLLM (uv tool).
Winget-Install 'Ollama.Ollama'
if (Have uv) { Info 'uv tool install litellm'; uv tool install 'litellm[proxy]'; if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure 'uv:litellm' } }
{{ end -}}

{{ if .installTunnelTools -}}
Scoop-Install @('ngrok', 'cloudflared')
{{ end -}}

{{ if .installIacTools -}}
Scoop-Install @('terraform', 'opentofu')
Winget-Install 'Microsoft.AzureCLI'
{{ end -}}

if ($script:Failures.Count -gt 0) {
    Write-Warning ("package install: {0} item(s) failed and were skipped:" -f $script:Failures.Count)
```
</details></tool-use>

```bash
git commit -F - <<'EOF'
feat: port create-only ~/.ssh/config skeleton from cross-platform dotfiles

Mirror the parent repo's SSH config approach, adapted for Windows OpenSSH:

- dot_ssh/create_private_config -> ~/.ssh/config: Include ~/.ssh/config.d/*
  plus conservative Host * keepalives. Create-only (never clobbers an existing
  config); private_ maps to an owner-only ACL so OpenSSH won't reject it with
  "Bad owner or permissions".
- config.d/00-defaults commented stub + config.d/01_git (GitHub over
  ssh.github.com:443, GitLab, commented multi-account / SOCKS5-proxy examples).
- Windows-specific: agent guidance uses the OpenSSH Authentication Agent
  service + //./pipe/openssh-ssh-agent (no SSH_AUTH_SOCK); ncat instead of nc
  for the proxy example; forward-slash IdentityAgent note for OpenSSH >= 8.9.

README gets a dot_ssh/ Layout row + SSH section; TODO.md records a deferred P3
item for a Windows ssh-agent profile fragment (parallel to 94_ssh_agent.zsh).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -1
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-10 06:49:16Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Render packages template and pwsh parse-check

```bash
set -e
TMPD=$(mktemp -d); mkdir -p "$TMPD/home"
chezmoi init --source="$PWD" --config="$TMPD/c.toml" --persistent-state="$TMPD/s.db" \
  --destination="$TMPD/home" --no-tty \
  --promptChoice='Role: workstation (full desktop) or minimal (shell only)=workstation' \
  --promptString='Your full name (git)=T Tester' \
  --promptString='Your git email=t@e.com' \
  --promptBool='Install coding agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex, Copilot CLI, SpecStory)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install GUI apps via winget (VSCode, Cursor, Notepad++, Terminal, Alacritty, PowerToys, Raycast)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install utility apps via winget (CPU-Z, GPU-Z, TreeSize, VLC, Everything, ShareX, HWiNFO)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install gaming apps (Steam)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install extra runtimes (rust, go, ruby) beyond node/bun/uv=true' \
  --promptBool='Install media CLIs (ffmpeg, imagemagick)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install local LLM tools (Ollama, LiteLLM)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install tunnel tools (ngrok, cloudflared)=true' \
  --promptBool='Install Infrastructure-as-Code tools (Azure CLI, Terraform, OpenTofu)=true' \
  --promptBool='Use China (GFW) mirrors for pip/npm/cargo/go/node=false' \
  --promptBool='Managed or corporate machine (skip org-policy-blocked apps like Tailscale and Grammarly)=false' \
  --promptChoice='Back up existing files before apply (smart|full|off)=off' \
  --promptBool='Enable PSReadLine vi editing mode=true'
chezmoi execute-template --config="$TMPD/c.toml" --source="$PWD" --persistent-state="$TMPD/s.db" \
  < .chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl > "$TMPD/r.ps1"
echo "=== glab / gh / az in rendered output ==="
grep -nE "glab|'gh'|AzureCLI" "$TMPD/r.ps1"
echo "=== pwsh parse check ==="
pwsh -NoProfile -Command "\$errs=\$null; [void][System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseFile('$TMPD/r.ps1',[ref]\$null,[ref]\$errs); if(\$errs){ \$errs | ForEach-Object { \$_.Message }; exit 1 } else { 'PARSE OK' }"
rm -rf "$TMPD"
```

用户问的是 Azure DevOps 是否有对应的 CLI 工具。Azure DevOps 确实有一个 CLI 工具，它是 Azure CLI 的扩展，叫做 `az devops`，需要通过 `az extension add` 命令来安装。

这个扩展提供了 `az devops`、`az repos`、`az pipelines`、`az boards` 和 `az artifacts` 等命令，用来管理 Azure DevOps 中的各种资源。由于这个项目已经安装了 Azure CLI，所以只需要添加这个扩展就能使用 Azure DevOps 的命令行工具。

对于 Git 认证，Git Credential Manager（GCM）处理 Azure DevOps 仓库的身份验证，它已经包含在 Git for Windows 和 Scoop 的 git 包中。Azure DevOps 也支持 SSH，使用 `ssh.dev.azure.com` 作为主机，所以可以在 SSH 配置中添加一个 Azure DevOps 的条目。

不过 Azure DevOps SSH 有个历史问题：它曾经只支持 RSA 密钥，并且在 OpenSSH 弃用 ssh-rsa（SHA-1）后需要特殊配置...

现在可能已经支持 Ed25519，但 RSA 仍然是推荐的。所以总结一下，有三个主要路径：管理 CLI 通过 `az devops` 扩展（已经在安装脚本中了），HTTPS 认证用 Git Credential Manager（Git for Windows 自带），SSH 连接到 `ssh.dev.azure.com` 时需要注意 ssh-rsa 的配置问题。

用户问的是 Azure DevOps 是否有对应的工具，就像 gh 和 glab 一样，答案就是 `az devops` 扩展。我可以直接回答这个问题，然后提供具体的后续步骤，比如添加 `az extension add azure-devops` 到安装脚本，或者提供 Azure DevOps SSH 配置片段。

I searched the web for information on Azure DevOps (ssh.dev.azure.com), SSH key types (ED25519 / RSA), and the OpenSSH config option PubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms (and its older name PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes). Key findings below with sources.

Quick summary (high‑value points)
- Microsoft’s Azure Repos documentation currently describes and documents RSA SSH keys for Azure DevOps (ssh.dev.azure.com) and shows how to generate/add RSA keys for Git access. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/repos/git/use-ssh-keys-to-authenticate?view=azure-devops))  
- Microsoft announced deprecation of the legacy ssh‑rsa (RSA+SHA‑1) signature scheme for Azure Repos and recommends migrating to RSA-SHA2-256 or RSA-SHA2-512 (or other modern algorithms). The announcement (DevBlogs) explains a phased deprecation and migration steps. (Feb 15, 2024). ([devblogs.microsoft.com](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/devops/ssh-rsa-deprecation/))  
- There is an active user request/issue asking Microsoft to add explicit ED25519 support for Azure DevOps Git SSH keys (developer community ticket), indicating ED25519 wasn't the documented/default on Azure DevOps at the time of those posts. ([developercommunity.visualstudio.com](https://developercommunity.visualstudio.com/t/Support-ED25519-Keys-in-Azure-DevOps-for/10961127?sort=recent))  
- If you encounter the error about "ssh-rsa not in PubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms" when connecting to ssh.dev.azure.com, common community workarounds include adding host-specific ssh config lines (Host ssh.dev.azure.com IdentityFile <key> IdentitiesOnly yes HostkeyAlgorithms +ssh-rsa PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes=ssh-rsa). That workaround has been posted on StackOverflow but relies on enabling the legacy ssh‑rsa signature and is only a temporary/compatibility measure. (Use with caution; ssh‑rsa/SHA‑1 is considered weak). ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/52985167/git-ssh-public-key-authentication-failed-with-git-on-azure-devops))  
- The OpenSSH project renamed PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes → PubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms (alias kept) and the newer name is available starting in the OpenSSH 8.5 era; using the newer name on older OpenSSH builds can produce "Bad configuration option: PubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms". Also, OpenSSH 8.8/8.x family disabled the old ssh‑rsa (RSA+SHA‑1) signature scheme by default for security reasons (recommend switching to rsa‑sha2‑256/512 or ed25519). ([github.com](https://github.com/openssh/openssh-portable/commit/ee9c0da8035b3168e8e57c1dedc2d1b0daf00eec))

Practical implications / recommended actions
- Best solution: create and use a modern key type supported by both client and server: either an RSA key that uses rsa‑sha2‑256 or rsa‑sha2‑512 signatures (ssh-keygen -t rsa‑sha2‑256 / rsa‑sha2‑512 where supported) or, if Azure DevOps accepts it for your account/org, use ED25519. Follow the Azure DevOps “Use SSH key authentication” guidance for adding the public key. ([devblogs.microsoft.com](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/devops/ssh-rsa-deprecation/))  
- If you are blocked by legacy ssh‑rsa failures (and cannot immediately rotate keys), you can (temporarily) permit ssh‑rsa on the client side via your ~/.ssh/config or on the server side via sshd_config using PubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms / PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes and HostkeyAlgorithms to add +ssh-rsa — but this is a compatibility/stopgap step and weakens security because ssh‑rsa uses SHA‑1. Prefer rotating to rsa‑sha2 or ed25519 instead. See OpenSSH release notes and community posts for details and caveats. ([openssh.org](https://www.openssh.org/txt/release-8.8))  
- If you add PubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms in ssh config and get "Bad configuration option", your local OpenSSH is likely older than the version that introduced that name; try the older alias (PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes) or upgrade OpenSSH. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72540142/running-ssh-command-in-the-cmd-shows-me-bad-configuration-option-pubkeyaccepte))

- Help you generate a rsa‑sha2 or ed25519 key and walk through adding it to Azure DevOps.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-10 06:52:12Z)**_

No dedicated `gh`/`glab`-style binary — Azure DevOps is driven through the **Azure CLI `azure-devops` extension**, so it rides on the `az` you already have (gated behind `installIacTools`) rather than being a separate package.

## The management CLI (the `gh`/`glab` analog)

The [`azure-devops` extension](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/cli/) gives you:

- `az repos` — repos, and `az repos pr create|list|show|checkout` for pull requests ([az repos pr docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/repos/pr?view=azure-cli-latest))
- `az pipelines`, `az boards`, `az artifacts`, `az devops` (org/project defaults)

It **auto-installs on first use** of `az repos`/`az devops`, or explicitly with `az extension add --name azure-devops`. No new scoop/winget package needed — just the `az` that's already in the installer.

## Git auth (separate concern from the CLI)

- **HTTPS** — handled by **Git Credential Manager**, bundled with Git for Windows / scoop's `git` (OAuth/device-code flow to `dev.azure.com`). Nothing to add.
- **SSH** — host is `ssh.dev.azure.com`, and there's a real gotcha that ties into the `~/.ssh/config.d/` we just built: Azure DevOps [deprecated legacy `ssh-rsa` (SHA-1)](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/devops/ssh-rsa-deprecation/) — use RSA with `rsa-sha2-256/512`, and **ED25519 is still not officially supported** ([open feature request](https://developercommunity.visualstudio.com/t/Support-ED25519-Keys-in-Azure-DevOps-for/10961127)). So an Ed25519 key that works fine for GitHub/GitLab may be rejected by Azure DevOps.

## Want me to wire any of this in?

Two small, optional additions I could make:

1. **Installer** — append `az extension add --name azure-devops` inside the existing `installIacTools` block (right after the `az` install), so `az repos`/`az devops` are ready with no first-use prompt.
2. **SSH** — add an `ssh.dev.azure.com` entry to `~/.ssh/config.d/01_git` (User `git`, plus a commented `rsa-sha2`/ED25519-caveat note) so it's documented alongside the GitHub/GitLab hosts.

Say which (if either) you'd like and I'll make the change + mirror docs; otherwise this was just the rundown.

1294

1297	        path: "{{ item }}"
1298	        state: absent
1299	      loop:
1300	        - /tmp/git-graph.tar.gz
1301	        - /tmp/git-graph
1302	
1303	  rescue:

1307	
1308	    - name: Clean up git-graph temp files after failure

1310	        path: "{{ item }}"
1311	        state: absent
1312	      loop:
1313	        - /tmp/git-graph.tar.gz
1314	        - /tmp/git-graph
1315	
1316	# --- tldr ---
1317	- name: Check if tldr is installed
1318	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Debian"
1319	  ansible.builtin.command: tldr --version
1320	  register: tldr_check
1321	  changed_when: false
1322	  failed_when: false
1323	
1324	- name: Check for system npm
1325	  when:
1326	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Debian"
1327	    - tldr_check.rc != 0
1328	  ansible.builtin.command: command -v npm
1329	  register: system_npm_check
1330	  changed_when: false
1331	  failed_when: false
1332	
1333	- name: Install tldr via npm (Debian/Ubuntu, system-level)
1334	  when:
1335	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Debian"
1336	    - tldr_check.rc != 0
1337	    - system_npm_check.rc == 0
1338	  become: true
1339	  tags: [sudo]
1340	  community.general.npm:
1341	    name: tldr
1342	    global: true
1343	    state: present
1344	  ignore_errors: true
1345	
1346	# --- tldr (user-level fallback) ---
1347	# Gate broadened to RedHat per pitfalls/centos7-noroot.md — but note this
1348	# uses `mise exec -- npm`, and `dot_config/mise/config.toml.tmpl` omits node
1349	# on oldEL (CentOS 7) per the glibc 2.17 baseline. On EL7 mise falls back
1350	# to system node (often missing or too old) and this task no-ops via the
1351	# `mise not found` branch. Live with it; tldr is non-critical.
1352	- name: Re-check if tldr is installed
1353	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
1354	  ansible.builtin.shell: command -v tldr
1355	  register: tldr_recheck
1356	  changed_when: false
1357	  failed_when: false
1358	
1359	- name: Install tldr via mise npm (user-level, no sudo)
1360	  when:
1361	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
1362	    - tldr_recheck.rc != 0
1363	  ansible.builtin.shell: |
1364	    if [ -x "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin/mise" ]; then
1365	      {{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin/mise exec -- npm install -g tldr
1366	    elif command -v mise >/dev/null 2>&1; then
1367	      mise exec -- npm install -g tldr
1368	    else
1369	      echo "mise not found, skipping tldr install" >&2
1370	      exit 0
1371	    fi
1372	  args:
1373	    executable: /bin/bash
1374	  changed_when: true
1375	  ignore_errors: true

=== direct find ===
./dot_config/shell/28_tldr.sh
=== cat via find ===
# 28_tldr.sh - tldr language preference helper (shared bash + zsh).
# Moved from dot_config/zsh/tools/28_tldr.zsh; `typeset -ga` (zsh global
# array declaration) replaced with a plain top-level array assignment that
# works in both shells.

# Language preference order for tldrf fallback.
TLDR_LANGUAGES=(zh_TW zh en)

tldrf() {
    command -v tldr &>/dev/null || return 127

    local -a lang_flags
    lang_flags=()
    local lang
    for lang in "${TLDR_LANGUAGES[@]}"; do
        lang_flags+=(-L "$lang")
    done

    command tldr "${lang_flags[@]}" "$@"
}

I searched for information about the tlrc / tldr clients and the behavior of the -L / --language flag (including repeated uses and fallback behavior). Key findings:

- tlrc (the official Rust tldr client) documents -L / --language as “Can be used multiple times. Overrides all other language detection methods. tlrc will not fall back to English when this option is used, and will instead show an error.”. This is on the tlrc manual page. ([tldr.sh](https://tldr.sh/tlrc/?utm_source=openai))  
- The tldr client specification requires that a command-line language option override other detection methods and that a client “must strictly adhere to its value, and MUST NOT show pages in a different language, failing with an appropriate error message instead.” (CLIENT-SPECIFICATION.md). ([gitea.puchstein.bayern](https://gitea.puchstein.bayern/DawnMagnet/tldr/src/commit/0c5e36285c26f133ee242b2d27e37292f88b2b49/CLIENT-SPECIFICATION.md?utm_source=openai))  
- The Python tldr client (PyPI) describes similar behavior: if --language is specified, it will attempt to use only that language; otherwise it falls back to TLDR_LANGUAGE, LANGUAGE/LANG, and finally en. ([pypi.org](https://pypi.org/project/tldr/?utm_source=openai))  
- Other clients (e.g., tealdeer / other implementations) also expose -L / --language; behavior is client-dependent but the client spec governs expected behavior. See the tealdeer manpage for its -L option. ([manpages.ubuntu.com](https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/noble/man1/tldr.1.html?utm_source=openai))  
- Package/docs indexes (docs.rs, Homebrew formula) point to the same tlrc behavior and to the client spec (confirming the authoritative source is the tldr client specification and the tlrc manual). ([docs.rs](https://docs.rs/crate/tlrc/latest?utm_source=openai))

Practical installation options for Windows
- Recommended / simplest (uses gh to manage the extension)

- Scoop (third‑party bucket)

  2) Then: scoop install gh-dash (only works if that bucket contains a gh-dash manifest). Note: this is a third‑party bucket, not an official Scoop main/extras package—review the manifest before installing. ([github.com](https://github.com/littleli/scoop-littleli))

```text
1:# Shell & environment
7:## Profile loading
19:| `20_aliases.ps1` | aliases + helpers (`reload`, `cas`/`cau`, git shortcuts) |
21:| `35_yazi.ps1` | `y` — launch yazi, cd to where you quit |
28:## Reloading
34:function reload { . $PROFILE }                    # re-source everything
35:function cas { chezmoi apply  @args; . $PROFILE } # apply, then reload
36:function cau { chezmoi update @args; . $PROFILE } # git pull + apply, then reload
46:## PATH: Windows vs Unix
65:### We change PATH without the GUI
106:## XDG base dirs on Windows
=== tail of shell.md ===
`AppData\Local` bundles *cache*, *state*, and non-roaming data. ("Roaming" only
actually roams under domain Roaming Profiles / Enterprise State Roaming — on a
standalone PC it's just a folder with that name.)

Rather than live with the ambiguity, `00_env.ps1` sets the XDG variables to
Unix-style paths, so **XDG-aware tools keep a tidy `$HOME` shared with the
macOS/Linux dotfiles**:

```powershell
$env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $HOME '.config'
$env:XDG_DATA_HOME   = Join-Path $HOME '.local/share'
$env:XDG_STATE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.local/state'
$env:XDG_CACHE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.cache'
$env:YAZI_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'yazi'  # yazi defaults to %APPDATA%
```

starship, atuin, zoxide, and yazi then read `~/.config`. Apps that **hard-code
`%APPDATA%`** and ignore XDG — VSCode, Cursor, Alacritty — stay in
`AppData\Roaming`, which is why the repo tracks `AppData/Roaming/alacritty/…`
and the backup script's allowlist names `%APPDATA%\Code`, `%APPDATA%\Cursor`,
and `%APPDATA%\alacritty`.

So in this repo `AppData\Roaming` is **not** "Windows XDG" — it's simply where
the non-XDG native apps land. The real XDG role is played by the `~/.config`
paths we set explicitly.
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./docs/shell.md`

```text
7	## Profile loading
8	
9	`$PROFILE` (`~/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`) is a thin
10	loader. It dot-sources every fragment under `~/.config/powershell/profile.d/*.ps1`
11	in sorted (numeric-prefix) order, then your untracked `local.ps1` last. All the
12	logic lives in the fragments; the loader stays boring.
13	
14	| Fragment | Does |
15	|---|---|
16	| `00_env.ps1` | XDG base dirs, `$env:EDITOR`, PATH |
17	| `05_mirrors.ps1` | China package mirrors (when enabled) |
18	| `10_tools.ps1` | `init` hooks: starship, zoxide, mise, atuin, fzf, direnv, tv |
19	| `20_aliases.ps1` | aliases + helpers (`reload`, `cas`/`cau`, git shortcuts) |
20	| `30_apps.ps1` | app control (`applaunch`/`appquit`/…), audio, clipboard |
21	| `35_yazi.ps1` | `y` — launch yazi, cd to where you quit |
22	| `40_copilot.ps1` | import the `copilot-proxy` PowerShell module |
23	| `90_psreadline.ps1` | PSReadLine (vi mode, history) |
24	
25	Modules under `~/.config/powershell/modules` are prepended to
26	`$env:PSModulePath` by the loader, so they import by name (e.g. `Copilot`).

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```ps1
# 28_tldr.ps1 — tldr language-preference helper.
#
# Native PowerShell port of the cross-platform dot_config/shell/28_tldr.sh.
# `tldrf` runs the tldr client (tlrc, installed via scoop) with a fallback chain
# of languages: tlrc accepts `-L` multiple times and tries them in order, so a
# page missing in zh_TW falls back to zh, then en. Plain `tldr` keeps its native
# behaviour. Override the order in your untracked local.ps1 if you like.

# Language preference order for tldrf fallback.
$TLDR_LANGUAGES = @('zh_TW', 'zh', 'en')

function tldrf {
    if (-not (Get-Command tldr -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { return }
    $langFlags = foreach ($lang in $TLDR_LANGUAGES) { '-L'; $lang }
    & tldr @langFlags @args
}

```

```

rm -rf "$TMPD"
```

## gh extension check — the finding

**The Windows repo installs zero gh extensions today.** The parent (macOS/Linux ansible, lines 1194–1243) installs three:

| Extension | Windows-compatible? |
|---|---|
| `dlvhdr/gh-dash` (PR/issue TUI) | ✅ **yes** — Go binary with Windows releases (`gh dash`). *But not in scoop main/extras* — only `gh extension install`. |
| `meiji163/gh-notify` | ❌ **no** — bash-script extension (needs bash + fzf); won't run natively in pwsh. |
| `remcostoeten/gh-select` | ❌ **no** — shell-script extension, same issue. |

So only `gh-dash` ports cleanly. Two caveats if I wire it into the installer: `gh extension install` needs `gh auth status` to already be authenticated (won't be on a fresh apply), and a `run_onchange` script only re-runs when its content changes — so a "skipped, not authed yet" won't auto-retry later.

All done — tasks cleared, four commits on `main`, everything validated off-Windows.

## Session summary

| Commit | Delivered |
|---|---|
| `d95972c` | `dot_ssh/` create-only SSH skeleton (`config` + `config.d/{00-defaults,01_git}`), Windows-adapted |
| `9ddb619` | `glab` (GitLab CLI) → scoop core |
| `5ec7608` | Azure DevOps (`az devops` extension + `ssh.dev.azure.com` host) + tldr (`tlrc`) + `tldrf` helper |
| `b25b8d4` | `gh-dash` extension (auth-gated, idempotent) |

## Answering "Azure DevOps 的 git 有相對應的工具嗎"
Not a standalone `gh`/`glab` binary — it rides on `az` via the **`azure-devops` extension** (`az devops`, `az repos`, `az repos pr …`). Wired into the `installIacTools` block (where `az` already lives), guarded so a fresh-install PATH miss is harmless. SSH host added too, with the key caveat: **Azure DevOps rejects Ed25519 — use an RSA key** there.

## gh extensions
Checked: the Windows repo had **none**. Of the parent's three, only **gh-dash** runs natively on Windows (Go binary); **gh-notify** and **gh-select** are bash-only and were deliberately skipped. gh-dash installs via `gh extension install` once `gh` is authenticated.

## Validation (all green)
- Template renders + `pwsh` parses; **PSScriptAnalyzer clean** (fixed one false-positive redirect warning by hoisting `2>$null` out of an `if` condition).
- `tldrf tar --foo` → `tldr -L zh_TW -L zh -L en tar --foo` (verified with a stub).
- `mkdocs build --strict` green (en + zh-TW); all cross-file doc mirrors updated per the repo's invariants.

## Two things worth knowing
- **`az` stays gated** behind `installIacTools` (your earlier choice) — so the `az devops` extension only installs when that toggle is on.
- **gh-dash won't install on a fresh box** until you run `gh auth login`; because `run_onchange` only re-fires when the script changes, the code comment documents the manual `gh extension install dlvhdr/gh-dash` / state-reset path.

The only remaining working-tree changes are the unrelated `.specstory` transcripts, still unstaged.
